stem: What is the amplitude of \( y=[[a]]\sin([[b]]x+([[c]]))+([[d]]) \)?
What is the period of \( y=[[a]]\cos([[b]]x+([[c]]))+([[d]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct period.
This inverts \( b \) into the numerator instead of the denominator.
This reports \( b \) itself rather than \( 2\pi/b \).
This uses the amplitude \( a \) in place of \( b \).
This doubles the correct period.
This multiplies instead of dividing by \( b \).

stem: What is the period of \( y=[[a]]\tan([[b]]x+([[c]])) \)?
solution: Unlike \( \sin \) and \( \cos \), the tangent function has period \( \pi \) (not \( 2\pi \)), so \( \tan(bx+c) \) has period \( \dfrac{\pi}{|b|} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the \( \sin/\cos \) period formula \( 2\pi/b \) instead of \( \pi/b \) for tangent.
This inverts \( b \) into the numerator instead of the denominator.
This reports \( b \) itself rather than \( \pi/b \).
This uses the coefficient \( a \) in place of \( b \); the period does not depend on \( a \).
This halves the correct period.
This uses the \( \sin/\cos \) period formula with an extra factor of \( b \).

stem: What is the sum of the maximum and minimum values of \( y=[[a]]\sin([[b]]x+([[c]]))+([[d]]) \)?
solution: The maximum is \( [[a]]+[[d]] \) (attained when the wave equals \( 1 \)) and the minimum is \( -[[a]]+[[d]] \); their sum is \( ([[a]]+[[d]]) + (-[[a]]+[[d]]) = 2[[d]] = [[answer]] \) — the amplitude cancels, leaving twice the midline.
distractor_rationales: This reports only the midline shift, not twice it.
This adds the amplitude on top of twice the midline instead of letting it cancel.
This adds the amplitude once instead of letting it cancel entirely.
This leaves an extra amplitude term instead of cancelling it.
This ignores the vertical shift entirely.
This confuses the midline shift with the coefficient \( b \).

stem: For what positive value of \( b \) does \( y=[[a]]\sin(bx) \) have period \( \dfrac{2\pi}{[[n]]} \)?
solution: The period of \( a\sin(bx) \) is \( \dfrac{2\pi}{b} \). Setting \( \dfrac{2\pi}{b} = \dfrac{2\pi}{[[n]]} \) gives \( b = [[n]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct value.
This reports the target period itself instead of solving for \( b \).
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This leaves a stray factor of \( \pi \) in the answer.
